summary: Gruber reviews the 2020 iPhone SE, finding it an excellent value that pairs iPhone 8 hardware design with the A13 Bionic chip from the iPhone 11 Pro. He details how the A13 enables surprisingly capable Portrait Mode photography from a single camera using monocular depth estimation, producing results comparable to the 11 Pro's TrueDepth-assisted system. He notes cosmetic improvements over the iPhone 8, particularly the black front face on the white model and the minimalist back panel. However, he candidly describes the frustration of returning to the pre-iPhone X interaction model after years with Face ID devices, concluding that the X-style gestures are irreversibly superior. He positions the SE as ideal for upgraders from older iPhones or Android, not for enthusiasts already on modern iPhones, and predicts it will be Apple's last home-button iPhone.
key_points: The $400 iPhone SE with A13 chip beats a $3,000 MacBook Pro in single-core CPU performance and significantly outperforms the $600 iPhone XR
The A13's neural engine enables monocular depth estimation for Portrait Mode that approaches iPhone 11 Pro quality from a single camera — a remarkable machine learning achievement
The iPhone X interaction paradigm is a one-way street: after years with Face ID gestures, Gruber never once instinctively reverted to home-button habits, only the reverse
Cosmetic refinements — black front face on white, centered Apple logo, pure white back — make the SE the canonical ideal of the iPhone 6/7/8 form factor
Gruber wagers this is the last iPhone Apple will ever make with a home button
money_quotes: So, yes, a $400 iPhone SE bests a $3,000 top-of-the-line MacBook Pro in single-core CPU performance.
Like the iPhone 5-style 2016 iPhone SE before it, the new iPhone 6-style iPhone SE feels like the canonical ideal of the form factor it embodies.
When a new design is clearly better than an old one, it's a one-way street mentally.
Once you get used to the post-iPhone-X interaction model, there's no going back.
Everyone who is going to put their new SE in a case and never remove it should take a moment to appreciate the purity of this design before they do.

The iPhone SE is an excellent value if you're fine with the smaller display and Touch ID instead of Face ID. It's an astoundingly good value if you flat-out prefer the smaller form factor and familiar Touch ID experience.
I would wager, heavily, that this is the last iPhone Apple will ever make with a home button and the old-style user interface.
They knew they were onto a winning idea with the new interaction design for the iPhone X when they started instinctively using the X-style gestures on the older iPhone, and never vice versa.
key_insight: The 2020 iPhone SE is a remarkable value proposition that pairs flagship-level silicon with a legacy form factor, but its existence also serves as a farewell to the home-button era that the iPhone X's superior interaction design rendered obsolete.
